name: derivative weights
statement_text:
Let
                                             c    A
                                                  b
denote the tableau for an s-stage Runge–Kutta method. Then the ‘elementary
weights’ Φ(t), the ‘internal weights’ Φi (t) and the ‘derivative weights’ (Φi D)(t)
for t ∈ T and i = 1, 2, . . . , s are defined by

                                    (Φi D)(τ ) = 1,                                            (312a)
                                                 s
                                        Φi (t) =    aij (Φj D)(t),                             (312b)
                                                      j=1


                                                      k
                        (Φi D)([t1 t2 · · · tk ]) =         Φi (tj ),                          (312c)
                                                      j=1
                                                      s
                                          Φ(t) =            bi (Φi D)(t).                      (312d)
                                                      i=1

This definition is used recursively. First Φi D is found for t = τ , using (312a),
then Φi is evaluated for this single vertex tree, using (312b). This enables
(Φi D)([τ ]), using (312c), and then Φi ([τ ]) to be found for each stage. The
order is built up in this way until (Φi D)(t) is known for any required tree.
Finally, (312d) is used to evaluate Φ(t).
  The notation Φi D is part of a more general scheme, which we introduce in
Subsection 387. In the meantime, D should be thought of as an operator to
be applied to Φi , which replaces the sequence of Taylor coefficient weights in
a stage value by the set of coefficient weights for the stage derivatives.

                                                        "i,j,k=1

             5                                           i,j,k=1 bi aij ajk ck
                                                        "s
             5                                           i,j,k,l=1 bi aij ajk akl cl




  An alternative formula for Φ(t), which uses the vertex and edge
characterization of each tree t, is given in the following lemma, which we
state without proof.
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
The definition introduces elementary weights, internal weights, and derivative weights for a Runge--Kutta method using its tableau $(c, A, b)$. These weights are defined recursively over rooted trees $t \in T$, with base case for the tree $\tau$.

Variables:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s$: number of stages in the Runge--Kutta method
    \item $c$: vector of abscissae $(c_i)$ for the Runge--Kutta method
    \item $A$: matrix of coefficients $(a_{ij})$ for the Runge--Kutta method
    \item $b$: vector of weights $(b_i)$ for the Runge--Kutta method
    \item $T$: set of rooted trees
    \item $\tau$: the unique tree with a single vertex (the root)
    \item $[t_1 t_2 \dots t_k]$: tree formed by joining the roots of trees $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k$ to a new root
\end{itemize}

variables.name: s

variables.name: τ
variables.definition: the unique tree with a single vertex (the root)

variables.name: [t1 t2 ... tk]
variables.definition: tree formed by joining the roots of trees t1, t2, ..., tk to a new root

preamble: The definition introduces elementary weights, internal weights, and derivative weights for a Runge–Kutta method using its tableau (c, A, b). These weights are defined recursively over rooted trees t in T, with base case for the tree τ.
